content:
{
  "title": "Counterfactual Prompting: Eliciting Model Self-Correction via Paired Contradiction Queries",
  "problem": "Large language models frequently generate hallucinated or non-factual content, especially in open-ended or ambiguous prompt scenarios. Existing prompting strategies often fail to robustly signal when the model is likely to err.",
  "motivation": "Reducing hallucinations is critical for trustworthy AI, particularly as LLMs are increasingly deployed in high-stakes and knowledge-intensive applications. Most work focuses on instructive or retrieval-augmented prompts, assuming that factuality is maximized by guiding the model toward 'correct' content. However, LLMs can recognize contradictions and inconsistencies in their own outputs, suggesting underexplored self-verification capabilities.",
  "method": "We propose a novel Counterfactual Prompting method: for any factual query, the prompt is paired with a deliberately contradictory or negated version (e.g., 'What year did X happen?' alongside 'What year did X not happen?'). The model responds to both, and a self-consistency check is used—factual answers are accepted only if the original and negated prompts yield mutually exclusive, confidently differentiated outputs. This approach leverages the model's ability to discriminate false from true statements, surfacing uncertainty or hallucination via inconsistencies between the paired responses.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate on QA datasets with strong factual ground truth (e.g., TriviaQA, Natural Questions), comparing our method to standard and chain-of-thought prompting. Metrics include factual accuracy, hallucination rate (manual and automatic), and calibration of model confidence. Statistical analysis will test whether counterfactual prompting improves factuality and reduces hallucinations."
}
